news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in AI-driven scientific discovery, energy-efficient brain-inspired computing, advanced aerospace software from NASA, and innovative AI models identifying key emerging technologies.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine des découvertes scientifiques basées sur l'IA, de l'informatique économe en énergie inspirée du cerveau, des logiciels aérospatiaux de pointe développés par la NASA, ainsi que des modèles d'IA innovants permettant d'identifier les technologies émergentes clés.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en el descubrimiento científico impulsado por la inteligencia artificial, la informática inspirada en el cerebro y energéticamente eficiente, el software aeroespacial avanzado de la NASA y los innovadores modelos de inteligencia artificial que identifican tecnologías emergentes clave.

articles.title: AI just discovered new physics in the fourth state of matter
articles.summary: Physicists have taken a major step toward using AI not just to analyze data, but to uncover entirely new laws of nature. By combining a specially designed neural network with precise 3D tracking of particles in a dusty plasma—a strange “fourth state of matter” found from space to wildfires—the team revealed hidden patterns in how particles interact. Their model captured complex, one-way (non-reciprocal) forces with over 99% accuracy and even overturned long-held assumptions about how these forces behave.
articles.content:
Physicists have used a machine learning approach to reveal unexpected details about how particles interact in complex systems. Their work focuses on non-reciprocal forces, where one particle influences another differently than it is influenced in return.

The findings, published in PNAS, come from a collaboration between experimental and theoretical physicists at Emory University. By combining a custom neural network with laboratory data from a dusty plasma, the team showed that artificial intelligence can do more than analyze data or make predictions. It can help uncover entirely new physical laws.

"We showed that we can use AI to discover new physics," says Justin Burton, an Emory professor of experimental physics and senior co-author of the paper. "Our AI method is not a black box: we understand how and why it works. The framework it provides is also universal. It could potentially be applied to other many-body systems to open new routes to discovery."

High-Precision Insights Into Dusty Plasma Forces

The study offers one of the most detailed descriptions to date of the physics governing dusty plasma. This system consists of ionized gas filled with interacting charged particles, including tiny grains of dust.

Using their AI model, the researchers were able to describe non-reciprocal forces with more than 99% accuracy. These forces are notoriously difficult to measure and model.

"We can describe these forces with an accuracy of more than 99%," says Ilya Nemenman, an Emory professor of theoretical physics and co-senior author of the paper. "What's even more interesting is that we show that some common theoretical assumptions about these forces are not quite accurate. We're able to correct these inaccuracies because we can now see what's occurring in such exquisite detail."

The team believes this method could be applied broadly to systems made up of many interacting components. These range from industrial materials such as paint and ink to groups of living cells.

The study's first author is Wentao Yu, who worked on the project as an Emory PhD student and is now a postdoctoral fellow at the California Institute of Technology. Co-author Eslam Abdelaleem also contributed as an Emory graduate student and is now a postdoctoral fellow at Georgia Tech.

The research was primarily supported by the National Science Foundation, with additional funding from the Simons Foundation.

"This project serves as a great example of an interdisciplinary collaboration where the development of new knowledge in plasma physics and AI may lead to further advances in the study of living systems," says Vyacheslav (Slava) Lukin, program director for the NSF Plasma Physics program. "The dynamics of these complex systems is dominated by collective interactions that emerging AI techniques may help us to better describe, recognize, understand and even control."

The Fourth State of Matter Explained

Plasma is often called the fourth state of matter. In this state, gas becomes ionized, meaning electrons and ions move freely and create unique properties such as electrical conductivity. Plasma makes up about 99.9% of the visible universe, from the solar wind streaming from the Sun to lightning strikes on Earth.

Dusty plasma includes additional charged dust particles and appears in many environments, from the rings of Saturn to the Earth's ionosphere.

On the Moon, weak gravity allows charged dust to hover above the surface. "That's why when astronauts walk on the moon their suits get covered in dust," Burton explains.

On Earth, dusty plasma can form during wildfires when soot mixes with smoke. These charged particles can disrupt radio signals, making communication more difficult for firefighters.

Tracking Particle Motion in 3D

Burton's lab studies dusty plasma and similar materials by recreating them in controlled experiments. Researchers suspend tiny plastic particles in a plasma-filled vacuum chamber to simulate more complex systems. By adjusting gas pressure, they can mimic real-world conditions and observe how particles respond to different forces.

For this project, Burton and Yu developed a tomographic imaging method to capture the three dimensional (3D) motion of particles. A laser sheet moves through the chamber while a high speed camera records images. These snapshots are then combined to reconstruct the positions of dozens of particles over time, allowing researchers to track their motion in detail.

Using AI To Understand Collective Motion

Nemenman, a theoretical biophysicist, studies how complex systems emerge from simple interactions. He is especially interested in collective motion, such as how cells move within the human body.

"General questions of how a whole system arises from interactions of tiny parts are very important," Nemenman explains. "In cancer, for instance, you want to understand how the interaction of cells may relate to some of them breaking away from a tumor and moving to a new place, becoming metastatic."

Compared to living systems, dusty plasma offers a simpler environment for testing new ideas. This made it an ideal case for exploring whether AI could uncover new physical principles.

"For all the talk about how AI is revolutionizing science, there are very few examples where something fundamentally new has been found directly by an AI system," Nemenman says.

Designing a Neural Network for Discovery

Building the AI model required careful planning. Unlike systems trained on massive datasets, this project had limited experimental data.

"When you're probing something new, you don't have a lot of data to train AI," Nemenman explains. "That meant we would have to design a neural network that could be trained with a small amount of data and still learn something new."

The team spent more than a year refining the design through weekly meetings.

"We needed to structure the network to follow the necessary rules while still allowing it to explore and infer unknown physics," Burton explains.

"It took us more than a year of back-and-forth discussions in these weekly meetings," Nemenman adds. "Once we came up with the correct structure of the network to train, it turned out to be fairly simple."

The final model separated particle motion into three main influences: drag from velocity, environmental forces such as gravity, and forces between particles.

Surprising Results and New Insights

After training on 3D particle trajectories, the AI successfully captured complex interactions, including asymmetrical forces between particles.

The researchers compare this behavior to two boats moving across a lake. Each boat creates waves that affect the other. Depending on their positions, these waves can push or pull the boats differently.

"In a dusty plasma, we described how a leading particle attracts the trailing particle, but the trailing particle always repels the leading one," Nemenman explains. "This phenomenon was expected by some but now we have a precise approximation for it which didn't exist previously."

The results also challenge earlier theories. One long standing idea suggested that a particle's electric charge increases in direct proportion to its size. The new findings show that while larger particles do carry more charge, the relationship is more complex and depends on factors such as plasma density and temperature.

Another assumption held that forces between particles decrease exponentially with distance in a way that does not depend on particle size. The AI model revealed that particle size does affect how quickly these forces weaken.

The team confirmed these conclusions through additional experiments.

A New Tool for Exploring Complex Systems

The researchers developed a physics based neural network that can run on a standard desktop computer. They believe it offers a flexible framework for studying many-body systems across different fields.

Nemenman will soon teach at the Konstanz School of Collective Behavior in Germany, where scientists study systems ranging from flocks of birds to human crowds.

"I'll be teaching students from all over the world how to use AI to infer the physics of collective motion -- not within a dusty plasma but within a living system," he says.

Even with these advances, human expertise remains essential. Scientists must design the models carefully and interpret the results.

"It takes critical thinking to develop and use AI tools in ways that make real advances in science, technology and the humanities," Burton says.

He remains optimistic about the future.

"I think of it like the Star Trek motto, to boldly go where no one has before," Burton says. "Used properly, AI can open doors to whole new realms to explore."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where AI was used to discover new physical laws in the fourth state of matter, dusty plasma. This represents a major advancement in physics and AI's role in scientific discovery, with broad implications for understanding complex systems in nature and industry. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights the interdisciplinary collaboration and potential wide-ranging applications, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: AI reveals new physics in the mysterious fourth state of matter
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study plasma, often called the fourth state of matter, which is an ionized gas found in places like space and wildfires. Dusty plasma is a special kind that includes tiny charged dust particles and behaves in complex ways that are hard to understand.

**What happened** – A team of physicists at Emory University used artificial intelligence (AI) combined with precise 3D tracking of particles in dusty plasma. They built a special AI model that learned how particles interact with one another, discovering new details about forces that do not act the same way both ways between particles. Their AI was over 99% accurate and corrected old assumptions about these forces.

**Impact** – This is one of the first times AI has helped scientists find new physical laws, not just analyze data. The research improves our understanding of dusty plasma, which appears in many places from Saturn’s rings to wildfire smoke. It also shows that AI can help study other complex systems, like groups of cells or materials used in everyday products.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to apply their AI method to other systems with many interacting parts. One scientist will teach others how to use AI to study collective behaviors, such as how cells move in the body. This approach could lead to new discoveries in biology, materials science, and beyond.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By combining AI with detailed experiments, scientists uncovered new physical laws in dusty plasma, opening new paths to explore complex systems in nature and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA révèle une nouvelle physique dans le mystérieux quatrième état de la matière
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient le plasma, souvent appelé le quatrième état de la matière, un gaz ionisé présent dans l’espace et les feux de forêt. Le plasma poussiéreux est un type spécial qui contient de minuscules particules de poussière chargées et se comporte de manière complexe.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de physiciens de l’université Emory a utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) avec un suivi précis en 3D des particules dans le plasma poussiéreux. Ils ont créé un modèle d’IA spécial qui a appris comment les particules interagissent, découvrant de nouvelles forces asymétriques avec plus de 99 % de précision et corrigeant d’anciennes idées.

**Impact** – C’est l’une des premières fois que l’IA aide à découvrir de nouvelles lois physiques, pas seulement à analyser des données. Cette recherche améliore notre compréhension du plasma poussiéreux, présent dans les anneaux de Saturne ou la fumée des incendies. Elle montre aussi que l’IA peut aider à étudier d’autres systèmes complexes, comme des groupes de cellules ou des matériaux du quotidien.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent appliquer cette méthode à d’autres systèmes avec beaucoup de parties en interaction. Un scientifique va enseigner comment utiliser l’IA pour étudier les comportements collectifs, par exemple le mouvement des cellules dans le corps. Cette approche pourrait mener à de nouvelles découvertes en biologie et en science des matériaux.

**En une phrase** – En combinant l’IA et des expériences précises, des scientifiques ont découvert de nouvelles lois physiques dans le plasma poussiéreux, ouvrant la voie à l’exploration de systèmes complexes dans la nature et la technologie.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA descubre nueva física en el misterioso cuarto estado de la materia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian el plasma, conocido como el cuarto estado de la materia, un gas ionizado que se encuentra en el espacio y en incendios forestales. El plasma con polvo es un tipo especial que incluye pequeñas partículas de polvo cargadas y se comporta de forma compleja.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de físicos de la Universidad Emory usó inteligencia artificial (IA) junto con un seguimiento 3D preciso de partículas en plasma con polvo. Construyeron un modelo de IA que aprendió cómo interactúan las partículas, descubriendo nuevas fuerzas asimétricas con más del 99 % de precisión y corrigiendo ideas antiguas.

**Impacto** – Esta es una de las primeras veces que la IA ayuda a descubrir nuevas leyes físicas, no solo a analizar datos. La investigación mejora la comprensión del plasma con polvo, que aparece en lugares como los anillos de Saturno o el humo de incendios. También muestra que la IA puede ayudar a estudiar otros sistemas complejos, como grupos de células o materiales cotidianos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean aplicar este método de IA a otros sistemas con muchas partes que interactúan. Un científico enseñará a otros cómo usar la IA para estudiar comportamientos colectivos, como el movimiento de células en el cuerpo. Este enfoque podría llevar a nuevos descubrimientos en biología y ciencia de materiales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Combinando IA con experimentos detallados, científicos descubrieron nuevas leyes físicas en plasma con polvo, abriendo caminos para explorar sistemas complejos en la naturaleza y la tecnología.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of glowing, charged dust particles suspended and interacting within a softly illuminated plasma cloud inside a transparent chamber, with subtle wave-like ripples symbolizing non-reciprocal forces between the particles, alongside an abstract, gently glowing neural network pattern intertwined with the particles, all rendered in natural, muted colors to evoke discovery and the fusion of AI and physics.

articles.title: This new brain-like chip could slash AI energy use by 70%
articles.summary: A breakthrough in brain-inspired computing could make today’s energy-hungry AI systems far more efficient. Researchers have engineered a new nanoelectronic device using a modified form of hafnium oxide that mimics how neurons process and store information at the same time. Unlike conventional chips that waste energy moving data back and forth, this device operates with ultra-low power—potentially slashing energy use by up to 70%.
articles.content:
Scientists have created a new type of nanoelectronic device that could significantly reduce how much energy artificial intelligence systems consume. The innovation works by copying how the human brain processes information, offering a more efficient alternative to today's power-hungry AI hardware.

The research team, led by the University of Cambridge, developed a modified version of hafnium oxide that functions as a highly stable, low-energy 'memristor' -- a component designed to replicate how neurons connect and communicate in the brain. Their findings were published in the journal Science Advances.

Why Current AI Systems Use So Much Energy

Modern AI relies on traditional computer chips that constantly move data between memory and processing units. This back-and-forth transfer requires large amounts of electricity, and demand continues to rise as AI becomes more widely used across industries.

Neuromorphic computing offers a different approach. Instead of separating memory and processing, it combines both in one place, similar to how the brain works. This method could cut energy use by as much as 70% while also allowing systems to learn and adapt more naturally.

"Energy consumption is one of the key challenges in current AI hardware," said lead author Dr. Babak Bakhit, from Cambridge's Department of Materials Science and Metallurgy. "To address that, you need devices with extremely low currents, excellent stability, outstanding uniformity across switching cycles and devices, and the ability to switch between many distinct states."

A New Approach to Memristor Design

Most existing memristors operate by forming tiny conductive filaments inside metal oxide materials. These filaments tend to behave unpredictably and often require high voltages, which limits their practicality for large-scale computing.

The Cambridge researchers took a different route. They engineered a hafnium-based thin film that switches states through a more controlled mechanism. By adding strontium and titanium and using a two-step growth process, they created small electronic gates, known as 'p-n junctions', at the interfaces between layers.

Instead of relying on filaments forming and breaking, the device changes its resistance by adjusting the energy barrier at these interfaces. This allows for smoother and more reliable switching.

Bakhit, who is also affiliated with Cambridge's Department of Engineeirng, explained that this design solves a major issue in memristor development. "Filamentary devices suffer from random behavior," he said. "But because our devices switch at the interface, they show outstanding uniformity from cycle to cycle and from device to device."

Ultra-Low Power and Brain-Like Learning

Tests showed that the new devices operate at switching currents roughly a million times lower than some conventional oxide-based memristors. They can also achieve hundreds of stable conductance levels, which is essential for analogue 'in-memory' computing.

In laboratory experiments, the devices remained stable through tens of thousands of switching cycles and retained their programmed states for about a day. They also demonstrated key biological learning behaviors, including spike-timing dependent plasticity: the process that allows neurons to strengthen or weaken their connections based on timing.

"These are the properties you need if you want hardware that can learn and adapt, rather than just store bits," said Bakhit.

Remaining Challenges and Future Potential

Despite the promising results, there are still obstacles to overcome. The current manufacturing process requires temperatures of around 700°C -- higher than what standard semiconductor fabrication typically allows.

"This is currently the main challenge in our device fabrication process," said Bakhit. "But we're now working on ways to bring the temperature down to make it more compatible with standard industry processes."

If this issue can be resolved, the technology could be integrated into practical chip-scale systems. "If we can reduce the temperature and put these devices onto a chip, it would be a major step forward," he said.

Years of Trial and Error Behind the Breakthrough

The advance came after several years of experimentation and many setbacks. Bakhit said progress finally accelerated late last year when he modified the fabrication process, adding oxygen only after forming the first layer.

"I spent almost three years on this," he said. "There were a huge number of failures. But at the end of November, we saw the first really good results. It's still early days of course, but if we can solve the temperature issue, this technology could be game-changing because the energy consumption is so much lower and at the same time, the device performance is highly promising."

The work was supported in part by the Swedish Research Council (VR), the Royal Academy of Engineering, the Royal Society, and UK Research and Innovation (UKRI). A patent application has been filed by Cambridge Enterprise, the University's innovation arm.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in brain-inspired computing that could reduce AI energy consumption by up to 70%, addressing a major challenge in AI hardware with broad implications for energy efficiency and sustainability. The research is detailed, focused on a single innovation, and highlights potential large-scale benefits for AI systems widely used across industries.
articles.personality_title: New brain-inspired chip could cut AI energy use by 70%
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) systems today use a lot of electricity because their computer chips move data back and forth between memory and processing units. This constant data transfer wastes energy and limits how efficient AI can be.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Cambridge developed a new tiny electronic device called a memristor, made from a modified material called hafnium oxide. This device works more like a brain by combining memory and processing in one place. It uses much less power—up to 70% less—because it avoids moving data around so much. The device also learns in ways similar to brain neurons, making it more adaptable.

**Impact** – This new memristor design solves problems common in earlier versions, which used unpredictable filaments and high energy. By switching states through controlled layers inside the device, it works reliably and uses extremely low power—about a million times less current than some older devices. This could make future AI hardware much more energy-efficient and capable of learning like the brain.

**What’s next step** – The main challenge now is to make the device at lower temperatures so it can be produced with standard chip-making methods. If the team succeeds, this technology could be built into practical computer chips, leading to AI systems that save energy and work better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new brain-like chip developed by Cambridge researchers could reduce AI energy use by up to 70% while enabling smarter, more efficient computing.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle puce inspirée du cerveau pourrait réduire de 70 % la consommation d'énergie de l'IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les systèmes d'intelligence artificielle (IA) utilisent aujourd'hui beaucoup d'électricité car leurs puces informatiques déplacent sans cesse des données entre la mémoire et les unités de traitement, ce qui gaspille de l'énergie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Cambridge ont créé un nouveau dispositif électronique minuscule appelé memristor, fabriqué à partir d'un matériau modifié nommé oxyde d'hafnium. Ce dispositif fonctionne un peu comme un cerveau en combinant mémoire et traitement au même endroit. Il consomme beaucoup moins d'énergie — jusqu'à 70 % de moins — car il évite de déplacer les données constamment. Il apprend aussi de manière similaire aux neurones du cerveau, ce qui le rend plus adaptable.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau memristor résout les problèmes des versions précédentes, qui utilisaient des filaments imprévisibles et beaucoup d'énergie. En changeant d'état grâce à des couches contrôlées à l'intérieur du dispositif, il fonctionne de manière fiable et utilise un courant extrêmement faible — environ un million de fois moins que certains anciens dispositifs. Cela pourrait rendre le matériel d'IA futur beaucoup plus économe en énergie et capable d'apprendre comme le cerveau.

**Prochaine étape** – Le principal défi est maintenant de fabriquer ce dispositif à des températures plus basses pour qu'il puisse être produit avec les méthodes classiques de fabrication de puces. Si l'équipe réussit, cette technologie pourrait être intégrée dans des puces informatiques pratiques, conduisant à des systèmes d'IA plus économes en énergie et plus performants.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle puce inspirée du cerveau développée par des chercheurs de Cambridge pourrait réduire la consommation d'énergie de l'IA jusqu'à 70 % tout en permettant un calcul plus intelligent et efficace.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva microchip inspirado en el cerebro podría reducir un 70 % el consumo de energía de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los sistemas de inteligencia artificial (IA) actuales usan mucha electricidad porque sus chips informáticos mueven datos constantemente entre la memoria y las unidades de procesamiento, lo que desperdicia energía.

**Lo que pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Cambridge desarrollaron un nuevo dispositivo electrónico diminuto llamado memristor, hecho de un material modificado llamado óxido de hafnio. Este dispositivo funciona parecido al cerebro al combinar memoria y procesamiento en un solo lugar. Usa mucha menos energía — hasta un 70 % menos — porque evita mover datos continuamente. También aprende de manera similar a las neuronas del cerebro, haciéndolo más adaptable.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo diseño de memristor soluciona problemas comunes en versiones anteriores, que usaban filamentos impredecibles y mucha energía. Cambiando de estado mediante capas controladas dentro del dispositivo, funciona de forma confiable y usa una corriente extremadamente baja — aproximadamente un millón de veces menos que algunos dispositivos antiguos. Esto podría hacer que el hardware de IA futuro sea mucho más eficiente en energía y capaz de aprender como el cerebro.

**Próximo paso** – El principal reto ahora es fabricar el dispositivo a temperaturas más bajas para que pueda producirse con métodos estándar de fabricación de chips. Si el equipo tiene éxito, esta tecnología podría integrarse en chips prácticos, llevando a sistemas de IA que ahorren energía y funcionen mejor.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un nuevo chip inspirado en el cerebro desarrollado por investigadores de Cambridge podría reducir el consumo de energía de la IA hasta en un 70 % mientras permite un cálculo más inteligente y eficiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing neural network made of delicate, interconnected glowing hafnium-based circuits shaped like abstract brain synapses, seamlessly blending organic neuron-like forms with smooth, layered crystalline structures, all rendered in soft earth tones and gentle blues to evoke energy efficiency and advanced, stable technology.

articles.title: NASA Releases Powerful LAVA Software to US Aerospace Industry
articles.summary: For years, NASA engineers have turned to a tool called the Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) framework to solve airflow challenges that could mean the difference between mission success or failure. When engineers need to know how a spacecraft will navigate re-entry or whether a new aircraft wing design will create enough lift, they […]
articles.content:
This simulation of the Artemis I launch generated by NASA’s Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) framework shows how the Space Launch System rocket’s exhaust plumes interact with the air, water, and the launchpad. Colors on surfaces indicate pressure levels — red for high pressure and blue for low pressure. Teal contours illustrate where water is present. NASA/Chris DeGrendele, Nguyen Ly, François Cadieux, Michael Barad, Emre Sozer, Jared Duensing, Timothy Sandstrom

For years, NASA engineers have turned to a tool called the Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) framework to solve airflow challenges that could mean the difference between mission success or failure. When engineers need to know how a spacecraft will navigate re-entry or whether a new aircraft wing design will create enough lift, they turn to LAVA.

NASA recently released this tool to the aerospace community.

LAVA is a computational fluid dynamics software package NASA developed to advance critical aerospace missions, harnessing the agency’s collective expertise. It helps predict how air moves around rockets, aircraft, and spacecraft with stunning accuracy.

The same computational tools simulating Mars landers, predicting launch environments, and optimizing aircraft for efficiency is now available to U.S. researchers, companies, and innovators.

“This isn’t only about releasing software; it’s about accelerating innovation,” said Jared Duensing, LAVA team lead at NASA’s Ames Research Center in California’s Silicon Valley. “When university researchers can run more complex simulations and when small companies can optimize designs with NASA-grade precision, we’re not only sharing tools, we’re unleashing potential.”

This video shows a simulation of the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket using NASA’s Launch Ascent and Vehicle Aerodynamics solver. For the Artemis II test flight, a pair of six-foot-long strakes were added to the core stage of SLS that will smooth vibrations induced by airflow during ascent. The green and yellow colors on the rocket’s surface show how the airflow scrapes against the rocket’s skin. The white and gray areas show changes in air density between the boosters and core stage, with the brightest regions marking shock waves. The strakes reduce vibrations and improve the safety of the integrated vehicle. NASA/Gerrit-Daniel Stich, François Cadieux, Michael Barad, Jared Duensing, Timothy Sandstrom, Derek Dalle

Big questions, fast answers

NASA has been using computational tools for years to predict how air will move around new aircraft or simulate the thunderous acoustic environment of a rocket launch.

Imagine watching your favorite show on a slow flip-phone versus loading it on a lightning-fast network in crystal-clear 4K high definition. That’s the kind of transformation LAVA brings to aerospace simulations. Complex problems that once took days or weeks now run in hours.

The LAVA software also is compatible with computer hardware employing specialized microprocessors known as graphics processing units (GPUs), which can run many tasks at the same time and reduce power consumption when compared to systems using traditional, more general-purpose central processing units. For traditionally costly simulation methods needed for NASA’s most complex aerospace applications, LAVA has yielded stand-out efficiency on NASA’s flagship GPU-based supercomputer, Cabeus.

But the real breakthrough is how LAVA makes the seemingly impossible routine. Aerospace engineers rely on “scale-resolving simulations” to capture high-fidelity renderings of phenomena that can have profound effects on missions, including pressure waves, turbulent swirls, and acoustic signatures. Those were once resource- and time-consuming. Now, LAVA runs them on modest computing resources, making them readily available and easy to produce, even for novice users.

This video shows a simulation of the flow over a scaled Common Research Model wing using NASA’s Launch Ascent and Vehicle Aerodynamics solver. This video highlights the large region of separated flow on the upper surface of the wing that forms due to the leading-edge ice. Particle tracers are injected near the leading-edge ice and advected downstream. Particles are colored by streamwise velocity, where red indicates lower velocity, and the increasingly lighter blue indicates higher velocity (with white indicating very high velocity). NASA/David Craig Penner, Jeffrey Housman, Timothy A. Sandstrom

At NASA, engineers have put those capabilities into action to help launch and land spacecraft on the Moon and Mars while driving innovation for the next-generation aircraft. When NASA needed to understand supersonic parachute deployment for Mars missions – something you can’t easily test in Earth’s atmosphere – LAVA provided critical insights.

When engineers had to predict how ice formations would affect aircraft performance, LAVA delivered answers on conditions that are critical for flight safety.

To help astronauts launch safely on Artemis missions, LAVA simulated the launch of Artemis I, enabling engineers to understand the Space Launch System flight environment in detail. Releasing the software means that industry will be able to harness those same capabilities, potentially applying them toward everything from large supersonic airliners to smaller delivery drones and air taxis.

The Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) team at NASA Ames is developing the capability to simulate supersonic parachute inflation by coupling several physics modules together. It couples computational fluid dynamics for the motion of the air as well as structural dynamics and contact mechanics for the deformation of the parachute. The capability could help reduce risk for upcoming interplanetary missions with atmospheric entry like Dragonfly (Titan) and DaVinci (Venus). The video shows snapshots from the fluid-structure interaction simulation of the third Advanced Supersonic Parachute Inflation Research Experiment (ASPIRE) flight test (SR03) used to validate the approach and develop best practices. NASA/Francois Cadieux, Michael Barad, Timothy Sandstrom

Three approaches, one framework

Most computational fluid dynamics software forces engineers to pick one approach, like being handed a hammer when you need an entire toolbox. The LAVA framework offers three options for generating meshes, or grids of connected dots used to predict the behavior of fluids (including air) in a simulation.

This allows users to switch between the meshes depending on a specific problem or use multiple mesh types to compare predictions. They also can use LAVA alongside other tools for analysis and optimization to improve designs.

Among many other NASA programs and projects, the work on LAVA was supported through NASA’s Transformational Tools and Technologies project, which works to develop new computational tools to help predict aircraft performance. The project is part of NASA’s Transformative Aeronautics Concepts Program under its Aeronautics Research Mission Directorate.

Ready to dive deeper into LAVA? Visit the NASA software catalog for access information and learn more about the tool’s computational capabilities through this seminar about LAVA.
articles.reasoning: NASA releasing the LAVA software to the US aerospace industry represents a significant technological breakthrough with broad positive impact. It enables faster, more accurate aerospace simulations accessible to researchers and companies beyond NASA, accelerating innovation in spacecraft and aircraft design. The article provides detailed context about the software's capabilities, applications, and potential benefits for aerospace safety and efficiency, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: NASA shares powerful LAVA software to boost U.S. aerospace innovation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA has long used a special computer program called Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) to study how air moves around rockets, spacecraft, and aircraft. This helps engineers solve tricky problems like how spacecraft re-enter the atmosphere or how airplane wings generate lift.

**What happened** – NASA recently made the LAVA software available to the entire U.S. aerospace industry. This means universities, companies, and inventors can now use the same advanced tool NASA uses to simulate airflow with high accuracy. LAVA runs complex simulations faster and uses modern computer processors called GPUs to save time and energy.

**Impact** – By sharing LAVA, NASA allows more people to create safer and more efficient aircraft and spacecraft. For example, engineers used LAVA to improve the Space Launch System rocket for Artemis missions and to study parachute deployment on Mars. Now, smaller companies and researchers can explore new designs and solve problems that once took weeks in just hours.

**What's next step** – The aerospace community can begin using LAVA to speed up innovation for projects like supersonic airliners, delivery drones, and future space missions. NASA will continue improving LAVA to simulate even more complex situations, such as parachutes opening at high speeds on other planets.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s release of LAVA software opens new doors for faster, safer, and smarter aerospace design across the U.S. industry.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA partage le puissant logiciel LAVA pour stimuler l'innovation aérospatiale américaine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA utilise depuis longtemps un programme informatique spécial appelé Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) pour étudier comment l’air circule autour des fusées, des engins spatiaux et des avions. Cela aide les ingénieurs à résoudre des problèmes complexes, comme la rentrée atmosphérique des engins spatiaux ou la portance des ailes d’avion.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La NASA a récemment rendu le logiciel LAVA accessible à toute l’industrie aérospatiale américaine. Cela signifie que les universités, entreprises et inventeurs peuvent désormais utiliser le même outil avancé que la NASA pour simuler le flux d’air avec une grande précision. LAVA exécute des simulations complexes plus rapidement et utilise des processeurs modernes appelés GPU pour gagner du temps et de l’énergie.

**Impact** – En partageant LAVA, la NASA permet à un plus grand nombre de créer des avions et engins spatiaux plus sûrs et plus efficaces. Par exemple, les ingénieurs ont utilisé LAVA pour améliorer la fusée Space Launch System lors des missions Artemis et pour étudier le déploiement des parachutes sur Mars. Désormais, les petites entreprises et chercheurs peuvent explorer de nouveaux designs et résoudre des problèmes qui prenaient auparavant des semaines en seulement quelques heures.

**Prochaine étape** – La communauté aérospatiale peut commencer à utiliser LAVA pour accélérer l’innovation sur des projets comme les avions supersoniques, les drones de livraison et les futures missions spatiales. La NASA continuera à améliorer LAVA pour simuler des situations encore plus complexes, comme l’ouverture des parachutes à grande vitesse sur d’autres planètes.

**En une phrase** – La mise à disposition du logiciel LAVA par la NASA ouvre de nouvelles possibilités pour des designs aérospatiaux plus rapides, plus sûrs et plus intelligents dans toute l’industrie américaine.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA comparte el potente software LAVA para impulsar la innovación aeroespacial en EE. UU.
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA ha utilizado durante años un programa informático especial llamado Launch, Ascent, and Vehicle Aerodynamics (LAVA) para estudiar cómo se mueve el aire alrededor de cohetes, naves espaciales y aviones. Esto ayuda a los ingenieros a resolver problemas difíciles, como la reentrada de las naves o cómo las alas de un avión generan sustentación.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA puso recientemente el software LAVA a disposición de toda la industria aeroespacial de EE. UU. Esto significa que universidades, empresas e innovadores ahora pueden usar la misma herramienta avanzada que NASA usa para simular el flujo de aire con gran precisión. LAVA realiza simulaciones complejas más rápido y usa procesadores modernos llamados GPU para ahorrar tiempo y energía.

**Impacto** – Al compartir LAVA, la NASA permite que más personas creen aviones y naves espaciales más seguros y eficientes. Por ejemplo, los ingenieros usaron LAVA para mejorar el cohete Space Launch System en las misiones Artemis y para estudiar el despliegue de paracaídas en Marte. Ahora, pequeñas empresas e investigadores pueden explorar nuevos diseños y resolver problemas que antes tomaban semanas en solo horas.

**Próximo paso** – La comunidad aeroespacial puede comenzar a usar LAVA para acelerar la innovación en proyectos como aviones supersónicos, drones de entrega y futuras misiones espaciales. NASA seguirá mejorando LAVA para simular situaciones aún más complejas, como la apertura de paracaídas a altas velocidades en otros planetas.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La liberación del software LAVA por parte de NASA abre nuevas puertas para diseños aeroespaciales más rápidos, seguros e inteligentes en toda la industria de EE. UU.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting depicting an abstract rocket ascending through layers of swirling air and water currents, with colorful pressure waves in reds and blues flowing smoothly around its sleek form, alongside translucent mesh grids and glowing computer-like patterns symbolizing advanced aerodynamic simulations enveloping the scene.

articles.title: Wikipedia-based AI model reveals the 100 technologies to watch
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00980-4Researchers mined the online encyclopedia to find the innovations that are gaining momentum fastest in science and industry.
articles.content:
3D printing is predicted to be one of the fastest-growing technologies this year. Credit: Stenko Vladislav Vitalievich/iStock via Getty

Machine learning, blockchain databases and 3D printing will be some of the fastest-growing technologies this year. That’s the prediction of the artificial-intelligence-powered 2026 Momentum 100 list of rapidly emerging technology, released by Australian researchers in December 2025. Soft robotics, augmented reality and ’omics — large‑scale, data‑driven studies of biological molecules such as DNA, proteins and metabolites — were also among the top-ranked technologies.

The inaugural Momentum 100 list was derived from an open-access data set called Cosmos 1.0, published in the journal Scientific Data1. To produce the data set, the study authors used a large language model (LLM) to extract information from thousands of Wikipedia pages about science and technology (see ‘Momentum leaders in the Nature Index’). The LLM then clustered the content on the basis of written and hyperlinked connections to create a map of the emerging-technology landscape.

The data set, which is available on the repositories Figshare and Github, can be filtered in various ways, adjusting indices such as a technology’s age and its Wikipedia pageview trends over time. (Figshare is owned by Digital Science, a firm operated by the Holtzbrinck Publishing Group, which has a share in Nature Index’s publisher, Springer Nature.)

“The Momentum 100 concept was to use the indices to filter emerging technologies and provide an interesting lens that we could repeat on an annual basis,” says Paul McCarthy, co-founder of the data-science consultancy League of Scholars in Sydney, Australia, which led the analysis.

The top emerging technology was a type of machine learning called reinforcement learning, in which a system learns by trial and error (see ‘Leaders in the Momentum 100’). This is a versatile mode of AI because it enables systems to make sequential decisions in complex, changing environments. It has so far been used in areas such as drug design2, drone racing3 and game-playing4, with AI models based on reinforcement learning beating top human players in chess, the strategy game Go, and shogi, or Japanese chess.

Blockchain technology came a close second in the Momentum 100 list. The topic has captured broad research interest beyond its cryptocurrency origins, as highlighted by several highly cited publications in the Nature Index between 2015 and 2025. The papers include one on swarm learning — a method that lets hospitals and laboratories collaboratively train AI on medical data without sharing patient information — which has been cited more than 800 times5. Blockchain technology has been applied to ensure the integrity of the data in food supply chains, clinical-trial records and renewable-energy generation.

Leveraging language models

To build the Cosmos 1.0 data set, McCarthy and his team used a pretrained language model called Wikipedia2Vec, which can turn Wikipedia articles into multidimensional number strings called embeddings, enabling researchers to do a geometric analysis of how closely connected certain Wikipedia articles are. “Embeddings encapsulate the contextual meaning of the article based on the words and sentences, but importantly, also the links they contain,” McCarthy says.

From a single Wikipedia ‘seed’ article titled ‘List of emerging technologies’, the team collated a list of almost 55,000 Wikipedia pages that were most similar to the seed article, then filtered these by article type to produce a list of more than 23,000 technologies and technology-adjacent concepts. “We then developed a range of indices around those technologies to characterize them in different ways,” McCarthy says.

The output was cross-validated by comparing it with other data sets, including academic publication and patent data.

Annual lists of emerging technologies are more than just fun talking points, says Daniele Rotolo, who studies emerging technologies at the University of Sussex near Brighton, UK. Such technologies have the potential to destroy existing industries and create new ones, he says, which makes them an important area for policymakers and industry leaders to keep an eye on.

“Worldwide, there is a great interest from governments and companies in these technologies because there is a strong expectation that they could change the status quo,” says Rotolo. “Especially with current geopolitical tensions, the interest in these technologies has become even more important because governments want to invest at the right time and develop a competitive advantage.”

Emerging technologies can also create ripples through the research sector, representing areas in which governments might increase research support through public funding, Rotolo adds.

Despite the imperative of identifying these technologies, forecasting their future effects is difficult, says Rotolo. “The problem is that at this early stage, we have few real traces in the traditional data” that can be used to determine whether they are poised for real-world impact, he says.

Momentum 100 leaders in the Nature Index An analysis of the leading Momentum 100 technologies in the Nature Index examines their influence on high-quality research publishing in 2025 and over time. The charts below are based on keyword searches across titles, abstracts and concepts in journals tracked by the index, performed in the Dimensions database curated by Digital Science. (Digital Science is operated by the Holtzbrinck Publishing Group, which has a share in Nature Index’s publisher, Springer Nature.) It is important to note that only primary-research papers in natural- and health-science journals were included in this analysis. The results are therefore skewed in favour of technologies such as ’omics, which encompasses many areas of biological study, including genomics, proteomics and metabolomics. Technologies such as blockchain and knowledge graphs — a type of data model that tracks connections between data points — appear more frequently in conference proceedings and applied-sciences journals, so will naturally have a much lower output in this analysis.

AI versus the experts

Because of this challenge, most annual emerging-technologies lists — including those created by the World Economic Forum (WEF), Stanford University and MIT Technology Review — are based heavily on expert opinion rather than pure data analysis.

McCarthy says that this is the point of difference for the Momentum 100 list — it’s based on trends in data, and does not incorporate expert opinion. “Our work was motivated by the idea of mapping technology from a more granular, bottom-up approach, using AI’s ability to reveal latent knowledge in large, complex systems,” he says. “We think there are some inherent benefits to this idea, compared with asking a small group of experts.”

According to Catherine Aiken, who leads the data-science team at Georgetown University’s Center for Security and Emerging Technology in Washington DC, the approach of McCarthy and his team streamlines what can be a slow and laborious process.

“In the six years that I’ve been dialled into this work, the approaches researchers use have been a bit stagnant,” she says. “How we pick an emerging technology and understand its context and potential implications is very expert-driven, very manual, very case-by-case.”

But in the past year or two, there’s been growing interest in using LLMs to extract more insights more quickly, Aiken says. “The Cosmos 1.0 approach is a useful addition to the field. Anything that tries something different to the expert-led, manual process is a useful contribution, because we’re trying to figure out where LLMs can plug in here.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a novel AI-driven method to identify rapidly emerging technologies using Wikipedia data, providing a data-based, scalable approach to track innovations with broad societal and industrial impact. This method offers a significant improvement over traditional expert-driven lists, potentially accelerating policy and research decisions worldwide.
articles.personality_title: AI analyzes Wikipedia to find 100 fast-growing technologies for 2026
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Researchers want to know which new technologies are growing quickly so governments and companies can make smart decisions. Usually, experts pick these technologies by opinion, which can be slow and limited.

**What happened** – A team in Australia used artificial intelligence (AI) to study thousands of Wikipedia pages about science and technology. Using a special language model called Wikipedia2Vec, they turned the Wikipedia articles into data points and grouped similar technologies. This helped them create the 2026 Momentum 100 list, showing the 100 fastest-growing technologies.

**Impact** – This list is different because it’s based on real data, not just expert guesses. It highlights important technologies like machine learning, blockchain, 3D printing, soft robotics, and studies of biological molecules called ’omics. For example, reinforcement learning, a type of AI, topped the list and is already helping in areas like drug design and game playing. Blockchain is also growing beyond cryptocurrencies, helping hospitals share medical data without risking privacy.

**What’s next step** – The researchers plan to update this list every year, giving policymakers and businesses a clearer, faster way to track new technologies. This tool might help governments decide where to invest research money or develop new industries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using AI to study Wikipedia, scientists created a new, data-driven list of 100 fast-growing technologies that can guide future research and innovation decisions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une IA analyse Wikipédia pour révéler 100 technologies en forte croissance en 2026
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chercheurs veulent identifier les technologies émergentes rapidement pour aider les gouvernements et entreprises à prendre de bonnes décisions. Habituellement, ce sont des experts qui choisissent ces technologies, ce qui peut être lent et limité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe australienne a utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour analyser des milliers de pages Wikipédia sur la science et la technologie. Grâce à un modèle appelé Wikipedia2Vec, ils ont transformé les articles en données et regroupé les technologies similaires. Cela a permis de créer la liste Momentum 100 de 2026, montrant les 100 technologies qui croissent le plus vite.

**Impact** – Cette liste est différente car elle repose sur des données réelles, et non sur des avis d’experts. Elle met en avant des technologies importantes comme l’apprentissage automatique, la blockchain, l’impression 3D, la robotique douce et les ’omics, des études de molécules biologiques. Par exemple, l’apprentissage par renforcement, un type d’IA, est en tête et aide déjà dans la conception de médicaments et les jeux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient de mettre à jour cette liste chaque année, offrant aux décideurs un moyen rapide et clair de suivre les nouvelles technologies. Cet outil pourrait aider les gouvernements à choisir où investir dans la recherche ou développer de nouvelles industries.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – En utilisant l’IA pour étudier Wikipédia, des scientifiques ont créé une liste basée sur les données des 100 technologies en forte croissance, utile pour orienter la recherche et l’innovation.
articles.personality_title_es: Una IA analiza Wikipedia para revelar 100 tecnologías de rápido crecimiento en 2026
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los investigadores quieren saber qué tecnologías nuevas están creciendo rápido para ayudar a gobiernos y empresas a tomar buenas decisiones. Normalmente, los expertos eligen estas tecnologías, lo que puede ser lento y limitado.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo australiano usó inteligencia artificial (IA) para estudiar miles de páginas de Wikipedia sobre ciencia y tecnología. Usaron un modelo llamado Wikipedia2Vec para convertir los artículos en datos y agrupar tecnologías similares. Así crearon la lista Momentum 100 para 2026, que muestra las 100 tecnologías que más rápido crecen.

**Impacto** – Esta lista es diferente porque se basa en datos reales, no solo en opiniones de expertos. Destaca tecnologías importantes como aprendizaje automático, blockchain, impresión 3D, robótica blanda y ’omics, que estudian moléculas biológicas. Por ejemplo, el aprendizaje por refuerzo, un tipo de IA, está en la cima y ya ayuda en diseño de medicamentos y juegos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean actualizar esta lista cada año, dando a los políticos y empresas una forma más clara y rápida de seguir nuevas tecnologías. Esta herramienta puede ayudar a decidir dónde invertir en investigación o crear nuevas industrias.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Usando IA para analizar Wikipedia, los científicos crearon una lista basada en datos de las 100 tecnologías de rápido crecimiento que puede guiar la investigación y la innovación.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an intricate, glowing network of interconnected Wikipedia pages represented as softly illuminated, floating tablets linked by delicate golden threads, with symbolic icons for machine learning (a stylized brain with circuits), blockchain (interlocking transparent blocks), and 3D printing (a small layered object forming in mid-air), all set against a calm, neutral background with natural, muted earth tones.
